data.title: IVV: U.S. Bellwethers In An Ultra-Low-Cost ETF Wrapper
data.description:
iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:IVV) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund offering ultra-low-cost exposure to U.S. bellwethers, or the 500 most generously valued and arguably the most capable companies that can be found in the U.S. equity universe.

Thanks to the simplicity and straightforwardness of its strategy, super low expense ratio, and performance that is infamously complicated to outmatch, IVV is something of a one-stop shop for U.S. equity investors with a wide set of goals, be it short-term gains or long-term wealth accumulation for retirement, etc.

Today, I would like to offer an in-depth analysis of this product that will cover the key aspects investors should consider before buying into it, including the details of the index creation process, performance it delivered in the past, composition of the portfolio, and risks this vehicle comes with.

IVV Basics

  • IVV was incepted on May 15, 2000.
  • In terms of categories, the ETF falls into the Large Blend sub-class within the U.S. Equity class.
  • BlackRock Fund Advisors is the investment adviser.
  • IVV tracks the S&P 500 index; S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC is the index provider.
  • IVV is one of the largest ETFs in the world, with assets under management of $591.28 billion as of June 7, 2025.
  • It is also one of the most liquid, with a 3-month average daily dollar volume of $4.42 billion.

What Does The iShares Core S&P 500 ETF Do?

According to the iShares website, IVV is managed passively, with the S&P 500 index being the cornerstone of its strategy.

However, an important remark here is that, as we know from the prospectus, BlackRock Fund Advisors "uses a representative sampling indexing strategy to manage the Fund," which essentially means that the overlap between the underlying index and the ETF's portfolio may be below 100%.

Established in 1957, the S&P 500 is sometimes equated with the U.S. market, and for good reason, as it captures a large group of the most valuable companies in the country.

As described by S&P Global:

The index measures the performance of the large-cap segment of the U.S. market. Considered to be a proxy of the U.S. equity market, the index is composed of 500 constituent companies.

Together with the S&P 400 and the S&P 600, with which it has by design zero overlap, this index is one of the components of the S&P Composite 1500.

What securities can join the index?

As we know from the methodology document available on the S&P Global website, the S&P Index committee decides which stocks will join the S&P 500 and which will go.

This bellwether index welcomes common stocks listed on a few eligible exchanges, including the NYSE, Nasdaq Global Market, etc. The company must be "U.S.-domiciled." At the same time, American Depositary Receipts, exchange-traded funds, limited partnerships, master limited partnerships, and a few other types of securities described in greater detail on page 7 of the methodology cannot compete for a place in the index.

For context, the Nasdaq-100 Index, which is tracked by the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), welcomes ADRs, so there are a few such securities in the QQQ portfolio, with AstraZeneca PLC (AZN) being an example worth mentioning.

Next, a company must satisfy the size criterion, with a market cap of $20.5 billion being the bare minimum. It is worth explaining that though shrinking market cap is one of the main reasons for a company's removal from the S&P 500 index (i.e., as a consequence of its lackluster performance, Celanese (CE) was removed from the index in March 2025 and transferred to the S&P 600, which measures the performance of the small-cap echelon), it certainly does not immediately get expelled when its market cap drops below that milestone. For instance, as of June 8, 2025, 138 companies in the index (3.48% weight) had a market cap below $20.5 billion.

Candidates are also screened for liquidity and public float.

Besides, there is a quality-focused ingredient, which is called 'financial viability.' Simply put, it means that to become eligible for inclusion, a company must report positive GAAP net income over the most recent quarter, plus the sum of the net income figures for the most recent four quarters must be above zero as well.

This is one of the top differences between this index and, for instance, the Russell 1000, another barometer of the U.S. market, which is tracked by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB). It is worth reminding here that despite its surging market cap, Tesla (TSLA) was loss-making for a long time, so it joined the S&P 500 only in December 2020. But as the Russell 1000 does not require positive net income, TSLA easily qualified for inclusion about a decade earlier, in September 2010, contributing to its gains.

Obviously, precisely like in the case of market cap, the 'financial viability' rule does not imply that upon turning loss-making, a company would immediately drop from the index and from the IVV portfolio.

To corroborate, as of June 8, 2025, 26 companies (1.82% weight) in the IVV portfolio had a negative trailing twelve months GAAP net income, with one example being Intel Corporation (INTC), with a 0.17% weight.

What weighting scheme is used?

The S&P 500 features the float-adjusted market cap-based weighting scheme. The main consequence of that is the most generously valued U.S. companies (i.e., the Magnificent Seven or the trillion-dollar league) climb to the very top of it.

Another evident consequence is that mid-cap names, if represented, have a microscopic weight in it and contribute just a few bps to its performance, if at all. For a fuller context, as of June 8, companies with a market cap below $10 billion accounted for just 0.38% of the IVV portfolio, as per my calculations. At the same time, mega-caps, which are valued at more than $100 billion, had a 73.2% weight, and the trillion-dollar cohort accounted for 34.2%.

The S&P 500 has a quarterly rebalancing schedule. However, adjustments tend to be minor, which is reflected in the ETF's turnover of just 3%.

For example, in the first five months of 2025, the index, and, hence, the IVV portfolio, have seen just five additions and five deletions (four stocks replaced in March in one in May). In June, no adjustments were made.

How Does IVV Fit In A Portfolio?

Thanks to IVV's time-tested, simple yet potent strategy, it should fit as a core holding in almost any portfolio focused on capital appreciation regardless of the timeframe an investor has set, be it a multi-decade portfolio strategy with a focus on a comfortable retirement or short-term gains.

Who Is IVV For?

IVV should work for investors seeking convenient, cheap exposure to the most valuable U.S. companies (or to the U.S. market as a whole), regardless of whether they use taxable or retirement accounts.

In other words, this product is for investors who, instead of beating the market, which is notoriously complicated even for industry juggernauts like hedge funds, would like to just benefit from its ups and wait through its downs anticipating that in the long term, even after a few steep declines, returns would be nothing short of robust.

And in fact, IVV's long-term performance (and so did the performance of the U.S. market) has been exceptional.

  • Over more than 25 years of its existence, IVV had just five calendar years with negative total returns, namely 2001, 2002, 2008, 2018, and 2022.
  • In 12 calendar years, its annual return exceeded 15%, chief among them being 2013 (32.29%).
  • $10,000 invested in IVV shortly after its inception grew to $65,161 as of the end of May 2025.

And even though its returns were less impressive compared to those of QQQ, it offered a much more comfortable risk profile. For instance, QQQ was much more volatile over the period, with a 22.6% standard deviation vs. IVV's 15.2%. And the Nasdaq 100 ETF had a maximum drawdown of 79.66% (during the dot-com bubble burst) vs. 50.79% of IVV (during the Great Recession).

A due remark here is that IVV is not a good choice for value investors. The problem is that as higher growth companies tend to climb to the key positions in the index, it is reasonable to expect IVV's valuations to be constantly inflated. For a fuller context, as of June 7, 2025, there were 95 stocks in this portfolio with a B- Seeking Alpha Quant Valuation grade or higher. But owing to the weighting, they accounted for just 8.5%, while those with a D- grade and weaker, including all main ten holdings except for BRK.B, had an 82.4% weight.

How Is IVV Built?

As of June 5, 2025, there were 507 holdings in the IVV portfolio. In the holdings dataset, 503 were marked as 'Equity' (99.7% weight), with others being cash, cash collateral & margins, and futures.

Owing to the weighting scheme of the S&P 500, a solid share of IVV's net assets are deployed to the ten key holdings (35.8%), with all of them being trillion-dollar companies, mostly from the information technology, consumer discretionary, and communication sectors that have been the locomotives of the U.S. market growth in the 2010s and into the 2020s.

This group (with a nuance being Alphabet, which, as in the case of QQQ, is represented by the Class C and Class A shares) is presented in the table below:

Ticker Weight Company Name Market Cap Sector
(MSFT) 6.88% Microsoft Corporation $3.496 trillion Information Technology
(NVDA) 6.79% NVIDIA Corporation $3.458 trillion Information Technology
(AAPL) 5.97% Apple Inc. $3.046 trillion Information Technology
(AMZN) 3.88% Amazon.com, Inc. $2.267 trillion Consumer Discretionary
(META) 2.97% Meta Platforms, Inc. $1.754 trillion Communication
(AVGO) 2.41% Broadcom Inc. $1.161 trillion Information Technology
(GOOGL) 1.94% Alphabet Inc. $2.114 trillion Communication
(BRK.B) 1.77% Berkshire Hathaway Inc. $1.065 trillion Financials
(GOOG) 1.59% Alphabet Inc. $2.114 trillion Communication
(TSLA) 1.58% Tesla, Inc. $950.633 billion Consumer Discretionary
(JPM) 1.45% JPMorgan Chase & Co. $738.489 billion Financials

Data from IVV and Seeking Alpha. Weights as of June 5, 2025. Market cap data as of June 7, 2025

At the same time, the bottom 100 stocks, including such well-known companies as Ralph Lauren (RL), Clorox (CLX), and Invesco (IVZ), account for just 2.1% and thus have a microscopic impact on IVV's returns, which vividly illustrates disproportions in allocations investors should pay attention to.

Regarding sectors, information technology dominates the portfolio, accounting for over 32%.

Sector Weight
Information Technology 32.08%
Financials 14.11%
Consumer Discretionary 10.30%
Communication 9.66%
Health Care 9.57%
Industrials 8.71%
Consumer Staples 5.77%
Energy 2.99%
Utilities 2.45%
Real Estate 2.12%
Materials 1.95%

Created by the author using data from IVV. Sector weights as of June 5, 2025

As we can see from IVV's sector composition as of May 12, 2014, which I obtained using the Wayback Machine, the role of IT in the S&P 500 has seen a momentous increase over the previous decade, while cyclical sectors like energy have seen their weights diminish.

Sector Weight
Information Technology 18.47%
Financials 16.00%
Consumer Discretionary 12.02%
Health Care 11.96%
Industrials 10.79%
Energy 10.55%
Consumer Staples 9.12%
Materials 3.53%
Utilities 3.05%
Telecommunication Services 2.49%
Other/Undefined 2.01%

Created by the author using data from iShares obtained using the Wayback Machine. Sector weights as of May 12, 2014. A nuance here is that the telecommunication services sector was replaced with communication services in September 2018 and thus no longer exists

One of the reasons for that was a prolonged period of ultra-low interest rates in the U.S. that provided solid support to tech companies, as their valuations reflect potential cash flows that they are yet to generate in the distant future, and interest rates directly impact them (i.e., they appear more valuable when rates go down and vice versa).

IVV's value, growth, and quality mix

Even though IVV is not supposed to represent any particular style (so it falls into the 'blend' category), what investors should expect to see under the hood regarding factors is small, if not diminutive, exposure to the value factor, almost perfect quality characteristics, and material exposure to growth. All these facts are the consequence of the S&P 500's weighting scheme that pushes the high achievers to the very top.

Metrics IVV
Market Cap $1,065 billion
EY 3.67%
Adjusted Earnings Yield 3.82%
P/S 8.44
EV/EBITDA 25.99
Adjusted EV/EBITDA 21.34
EPS Fwd 17.41%
Revenue Fwd 11.79%
EBITDA Fwd 15.59%
ROA 14.83%
Adjusted ROE 20.38%
Quant Valuation B- or higher 8.50%
Quant Growth B- or higher 43.01%
Quant Profitability B- or higher 94.40%

Calculated by the author using data from IVV and Seeking Alpha. Financial data as of June 7, 2025. For the adjusted weighted average EY, loss-making companies were removed. For the adjusted WA EV/EBITDA, companies with four- and triple-digit figures were removed as well

For context, NVDA, IVV's second-largest holding with a 6.79% weight, is one of the main contributors to IVV's quality characteristics thanks to its A+ Profitability grade. It has also boosted its weighted average growth rates, and, inevitably, contributed to its generous valuation, which is confirmed by the metrics below:

Metric NVDA
EPS Fwd 64.12%
EBITDA Fwd 63.44%
Revenue Fwd 60.28%
P/E TTM 45.71
P/S TTM 23.39
EV/EBITDA TTM 38.69

Data from Seeking Alpha

IVV Risks

By design and unlike ETFs that feature an equal weighting method or other schemes that help to distribute risks more evenly, IVV is mostly dependent on the most expensive U.S. companies, which, for the most part, fall into the long-duration equity category, which means they are rather sensitive to the macro environment and especially to the shifts in monetary policy. For instance, higher interest rates that could be the consequence of the Fed trying to tame inflation could trigger long and painful bear markets. Recessions (or, better to say, investors' views on the possibility of a recession) could also result in IVV entering the bear market territory.

So even though IVV had delivered a 7.79% annualized return from June 2000–May 2025, it still had rather lackluster days, months, and years, with a few examples being:

  • April 4, 2025, when IVV declined by about 6% amid the news related to tariffs.
  • Four months, namely September 2002, October 2008, February 2009, and March 2020, when the monthly return was weaker than -10%.
  • Five calendar years (plus seven months in 2000) with a negative total return, namely 2001, 2002 (amid the dot-com bubble burst), 2008 (the Great Recession), 2018 (the trade war), and 2022 (the bear market in part triggered by the end of the era of the ultra-low interest rates).

IVV Pros & Cons

Pros

  • IVV offers exposure to the key U.S. companies that comes with an ultra-low expense ratio of just 0.03%.
  • IVV has solid quality and growth characteristics thanks to the market cap weighting that allows the most expensive and most efficient names to climb to the key positions in the index and the portfolio.
  • It has a more comfortable risk profile than, for instance, the tech-heavy QQQ, thanks to its breadth and depth of exposure.
  • IVV has exceptional long-term performance, which, even though it obviously does not guarantee anything in the future, tells a lot about its hypothetical potential.

Cons

  • Beating the market with IVV is impossible, as IVV is, in essence, supposed to track the market itself.
  • As its risk is lower than that of growth-focused ETFs, with one example being QQQ, reward is smaller as well.
  • IVV has disproportions in allocations. It is overexposed to just a handful of companies and the IT sector. This, combined with small allocation to defensive sectors (i.e., health care, utilities, and consumer staples), which shrank during the previous decade, adds to risks.
  • IVV has a perennial valuation issue, which means its decline during periods of market stress should be deeper than those of ETFs focused on undervalued or less volatile stocks.
  • The ETF has almost no small and mid-cap exposure, so investors interested in this echelon should consider the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) and the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH).
  • Its small dividend yield of just 1.29% (as of June 9, 2025) makes it a suboptimal pick for dividend-focused investors.

IVV Peer Comparison

IVV's primary peers could be divided roughly into three groups:

  • Those tracking the S&P 500,
  • Those tracking recalibrated versions of the S&P 500,
  • And those offering exposure to the U.S. market but based on the indices other than the S&P 500 or its augmented versions.

The first group is comprised of the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY), the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and the SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500® ETF (SPLG).

SPY is the oldest as it was incepted in 1993, while SPLG is the youngest—even though it was incepted in 2005, it started tracking the S&P 500 only in 2020. VOO was launched in September 2010.

In terms of AUM, SPY is the leader, though VOO and IVV are rather close.

ETF AUM
SPY $609.86 billion
VOO $608 billion*
IVV $591.28 billion
SPLG $69.27 billion

Data from the ETFs. AUM as of June 9, 2025. * the figure is 'Share class total net assets' as per the VOO website. 'Fund total net assets' were $1.3 trillion as of April 30, 2025

Performance-wise, differences are rather minor and are for the most part driven by the expense ratios.

ETF Expense Ratio
SPLG 0.02%
IVV 0.03%
VOO 0.03%
SPY 0.0945%

Data from Seeking Alpha

More specifically, from October 2010–May 2025, VOO delivered the highest annualized return in the group, with IVV in second place and SPY in third.

Metric IVV SPY VOO
Start Balance $10,000 $10,000 $10,000
End Balance $67,590 $67,329 $67,871
Annualized Return 13.92% 13.89% 13.95%
Standard Deviation 14.23% 14.21% 14.21%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer

And from February 2020–May 2025, SPLG was the best-performing ETF in this group thanks to its 0.02% ER.

Metric IVV SPY VOO SPLG
Start Balance $10,000 $10,000 $10,000 $10,000
End Balance $19,807 $19,831 $19,860 $19,881
Annualized Return 13.67% 13.70% 13.73% 13.75%
Standard Deviation 18.09% 18.04% 18.07% 18.05%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer

The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP)

RSP is the key representative of the second group. With the S&P 500® Equal Weight Index being the basis for its strategy, RSP's main idea is to eliminate risks stemming from the disproportions of the S&P 500 index (i.e., overreliance on the most expensive companies and the tech sector) by weighting all its constituents equally.

However, it underperformed IVV from May 2003–May 2025, and it was surprisingly more volatile despite theoretically having better risk dispersion.

ETF Annualized Return Standard Deviation
IVV 10.86% 14.57%
RSP 10.82% 16.79%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer

Also, the main contributor here was RSP's outstanding performance in 2009 (44.6% vs. IVV's 26.6%). So over other periods, for example, over June 2015–May 2025, its annualized return was considerably weaker:

ETF Annualized Return Standard Deviation
IVV 12.77% 15.49%
RSP 9.83% 16.91%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer

There are other disadvantages, including its higher turnover of 21% and a much higher ER compared to IVV, 0.20%. RSP is also considerably smaller than the iShares ETF, with an AUM of $71.55 billion.

The iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB)

IWB is one of the main ETFs from the third group. This fund has been tracking the Russell 1000 index since its inception on May 15, 2000.

Since its launch, IWB has amassed a rather considerable AUM of $40.3 billion, yet it is clear that it is substantially less popular than the S&P 500-tracking ETFs, which in part can be explained by its higher expense ratio of 0.15%.

While the Russell 1000 has about 2x more components, it would be a mistake to think that IWB offers exposures radically different from those of IVV. For context, as of June 9, 2025, the S&P 500 constituents (hence, IVV's holdings) accounted for 91.5% of IWB's portfolio. So it is reasonable to expect only modest differences in returns.

IWB slightly outperformed IVV from June 2000–May 2025, though at the expense of higher volatility:

ETF Annualized Return Standard Deviation
IVV 7.79% 15.18%
IWB 7.9% 15.48%

Data from Portfolio Visualizer

Bottom Line

The iShares Core S&P 500 ETF offers exposure to the 500 most valuable U.S. companies, which are frequently referred to as the U.S. market itself. The design of the S&P 500 index secures exceptional quality characteristics of the IVV portfolio, as well as healthy growth exposure. At the same time, valuation is a perennial concern. So while IVV has proved that it is capable of delivering strong returns over the long term, it is always worth remembering that its top-heaviness makes it prone to steep declines during stressful periods.

This article answers these three main questions about IVV:

  1. How does IVV's expense ratio compare to other S&P 500 ETFs?
  2. Is IVV a good way to gain overall market exposure?
  3. Does IVV do a good job of providing diversification?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: U.S. Tariffs Here To Stay, Trade Deals 'Largely Symbolic'
data.description:
By Inga Fechner

In recent weeks, two major developments have shaped the trade landscape: a US court ruling questioning the legality of tariffs imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), and the doubling of tariffs on steel, aluminium, and related derivatives, effective from 4 June.

Despite these major developments, we believe that the average tariff rate is unlikely to shift significantly, as other tariff measures are expected to offset any reductions resulting from trade deals or court rulings.

The US Court of International Trade ruled on 28 May that President Donald Trump exceeded his authority using the 1977 IEEPA to impose tariffs. Directly affected by this ruling are the 10-30% tariffs on China, Canada, Mexico, and the rest of the world. A stay is currently in place pending appeal, following the US administration’s immediate objection to the initial court ruling. The Court of Appeal has scheduled arguments for 31 July, meaning that tariffs under currently-used laws can stay in effect at least until then.

Despite the legal setback, the tariff landscape is unlikely to shift

Even if the IEEPA tariffs are ultimately overturned, we do not anticipate a significant shift in the overall tariff landscape. For once, there are fallback options such as Section 122 (Trump can impose tariffs of up to 15% or quotas on all imports for up to 150 days to address balance-of-payments deficits. Extending tariffs beyond 150 days requires Congressional approval) or Section 338 (the president can impose new or additional duties on imports from a foreign country of up to 50%, or exclude its products from importation if the foreign country imposes unreasonable charges or discriminates against US commerce).

We still expect Section 232 and Section 301 tariffs to intensify over the next few months. While individual sectoral country tariffs and subsequent reductions are possible, most sectoral tariffs will stay irrespective of trade deals due to national security reasons, especially if tariffs under IEEPA are struck down. We expect that:

  • Car tariffs will remain at a minimum of 10%, depending on trade deals, but for the majority of trade partners will remain at 25%.
  • Steel tariffs will remain at 50% and will not fall back to 25%.
  • Aluminium tariffs, however, are more likely to fall back to 25% as US production is pretty much limited (read our take here).
  • Other sectoral tariffs will most likely come in 3Q/4Q, probably in the range of 10-25% (copper, trucks, cranes, aircraft, pharma, etc.)

No US appetite for reciprocity with tariff revenue remaining strong

In general, we expect higher tariffs to stay. US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick made clear during a recent Senate Appropriations Committee hearing that the US is not looking for real reciprocity. Judging from his statements, the US is not interested in zero-for-zero deals or eliminating all trade barriers, as this would undermine the US agenda to generate new revenues and gain relative advantages vis-à-vis its trading partners.

Up to 30 April, revenues under IEEPA tariffs have totalled $13.7bn, while tariff revenues under Section 201, 232 or 301 duty assessments total $26.8bn for the 2025 fiscal year (October 2024 to September 2025).

Trade remedy duties on imported goods via IEEPA (as of 30 April, in $bn)

Trade deals: ‘showcase’ or the ‘real deal’?

While a temporary tariff truce between the US and its trade partners is in place – ideally lasting until 8/9 July for most partners and 12 August for China – ongoing trade talks remain clouded by uncertainty. Several countries are engaged in negotiations with the US, but the nature and substance of any potential trade deal remain unclear, largely due to the lack of a defined US position.

Talks with the EU and China are reportedly progressing, with the US and China having reached a framework on 10 June. Yet there is no clarity on what a final agreement might entail. The risk of talks breaking down persists, and in such a scenario, the EU, Canada, and other stakeholders are preparing to implement (additional) retaliatory measures. Current discussions with China are centred on technology export controls and rare earths, and both sides have agreed to ease some of these export restrictions, though specific details have not been disclosed. Meanwhile, a limited agreement with India is anticipated before the July deadline.

Overall, we expect most outcomes to be ‘showcase’ deals – agreements crafted for political optics rather than meaningful economic change. The US-UK case exemplifies the core issue: talks are happening successfully, but delivery is not.

It also means that by year-end, we do not expect the effective average tariff rate to be much different from its current value, meaning that the average effectively applied tariff rate will stay around its current level, i.e.

  • EU: 10-15%
  • China: 50%
  • World: 10-15%

A reduction in tariff lines via trade deals or court rulings will be offset by other tariff measures.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
