data.title: Edison International Remains At The Top Spot Of The Utility Sector
data.description:
Amid an uncertain macro environment and market volatility, the utility sector has emerged as one of the top-performing areas since the beginning of the year, with a 7% gain. In my recent article on the utility companies, I set up a scoring approach, which ranked the stocks in the Utilities Select Sector based on the analysis of the key financial fundamentals. Following the first-quarter earnings, the utility sector booked 6.1% earnings growth, with 65% of the companies beating the guidance. I believe that utility stocks remain attractive due to the stable dividend yield potential, and with this follow-up, I decided to update the approach.

*The table lists only the top 10 companies out of 31 overall in the score list. The lower the final score, the higher the company is ranked in the selection according to the specific metric. If you want additional information regarding the score of the stocks in the Utility Select Sector not included in the table, feel free to ask me in the comment section.

Note that the final score of a company is determined by weighting the dividend yield (20% weight), FCF margin (20% weight), net debt to EBITDA (20% weight), long-term growth forecast (10% weight), 12-month potential (10% weight), and valuation by P/E ratio (20% weight). All the metrics in the table are based on forward analysts’ estimates apart from ND/EBITDA and CFO/Capex, which are estimated with trailing data.

The result is that Edison International (NYSE:EIX) still commands the most attractive score. The Eaton fire investigation remains a significant risk factor for the company, but let’s dive into some details to figure out why EIX remains at the top stair.

Edison International

Edison International delivered $1.37 earnings per share for the first quarter of 2025, which represents a 21.2% growth on an annual basis. The company is yet to receive the 2025 GRC proposed decision, which stipulates that the readings would be updated. Hence, EIX recognized revenue based on last year’s revenue requirements, adjusted for the lower authorized ROE. Still, the executives remain confident in meeting the FY and mid-term guidance.

However, the main concern for the company remains the Eaton fire investigation. The reason for the blaze is still unspecified, and the SCE’s equipment implication with the ignition has not been determined yet. However, there is no evidence pointing to a third party as well, which made executives suppose that the company would incur material losses.

At the same time, the regulatory environment continues to be favorable, as SCE made progress across numerous proceedings. The utility achieved resolution on the TKM matter with $1.6 billion cost recovery, as well as reached important milestones with the Woolsey proceeding for $5.4 billion. SCE also filed its NextGen ERP, requesting about $1.1 billion, which is not included in the capital and rate base projections. In addition, the utility filed a 2026 cost of capital application for a higher authorized ROE of 11.75% (vs. 10.33% in 2025) with updated costs of debt and preferred equity, based on risks that differentiate California utilities from their peers in other jurisdictions.

Looking at the EIX performance, the stock price slid by 37% since the beginning of the year. The stock price also plunged by 14% since my previous writing, mainly due to ongoing litigation related to the Eaton fire and the pending GRC.

Regarding the AB 1054 framework, SCE continues to be committed to the wildfire mitigation processes. The utility plans to spend more than $6.2 billion in the 2026-2028 period on the installation of 440 circuit miles of covered conductor lines and 260 circuit miles of underground distribution lines. However, the wildfire risk and the potential involvement of SCE’s equipment raise the question of whether the AB 1054 framework would be sufficient. Last time I wrote that:

The AB 1054 framework allows California-based IOUs to tap into a $21 billion fund if their equipment is found to be responsible for a blaze and the utility company acted responsibly. Although, SCE would draw from its $1 billion self-insurance fund before addressing the state fund. In case utility actions are found to be imprudent, the liability cap for SCE to reimburse the fund is capped at $3.9 billion.

Still, the management of EIX remains confident in the wildfire fund, engaging with regulators to enhance the AB 1054 framework.

The company reaffirmed its near-term capital deployment program, which stipulates an 8% growth on average in the rate base. However, the additional capital opportunities in the company’s distribution system, along with FERC transmission spending, account for more than $5 billion, offering a substantial upside. That’s why executives remain confident in meeting the 2025 EPS guidance and delivering up to 7% core EPS growth through 2028.

Moreover, the utility is awaiting a proposed decision on the 2025 GRC, which is expected to bring a $1.9 billion increase in revenue requirements for the year. Despite the lower authorized ROE, the rate base is planned to increase prominently from a year ago, or up by 15%. As a result, I expect the executives to come up with revised upwards earnings guidance, as well as capital and rate base projections. The latter could be due to the crucial need for incremental grid investments for wildfire mitigation reasons, which is especially reasonable since the Eaton blaze.

The strong earnings provide a clear visibility for at least a $3.30 dividend payout to shareholders in 2025, which implies a 6.5% forward dividend yield. As a result, the company claimed to be #1 on the list by dividend policy criteria, followed by AES Corporation with a yield of 6.2%.

But what keeps EIX still on the top spot is the fundamental background. The company remains #5 according to the profitability measure, expecting to generate an FCF margin of 3.4%. The stable earnings growth and profitability lend a good indication of the company’s capability to pay and potentially increase dividends in the future. At the same time, EIX issued $3.5 billion in long-term debt during the first quarter, and the company now ranks #19 according to the net debt to EBITDA measure.

The most prominent change came from the long-term growth prospects. EIX slips from #7 down to #24 in the ranking. This could be explained by positive developments around the nuclear operators, who are securing a PPA agreement for AI data center workloads. Meanwhile, the main growth factor for SCE utility remains its capital deployment program.

The market continues to be positive regarding the EIX stock, as the analyst forecasts provide for a 34% potential gain over the 12-month horizon. Although the risks from the Eaton fire are tilted to the downside, the potential liabilities remain non-estimable so far due to the requirement for thorough regulatory investigations, which could take more than a 12-month horizon.

And looking at the valuation, the forward P/E ratio of EIX stands at 8.3x (vs. 9.5x in the previous article), which remains the second lowest and is considerably below the average ratio of 18.5x in the selection. In addition, the company is currently trading at a circa 40% discount to its 3Y average P/E of 14x.

Investment takeaways

Following the first-quarter results, the EIX remains the top pick in the utility sector according to my approach. The company is bound with a significant risk due to the Eaton fire investigation, with management acknowledging the utility’s equipment could be found involved. However, the strong fundamental appeal of Edison International remains intact, as the stock offers the highest dividend yield, backed by solid profitability and low valuation. The management remains confident in the AB 1054 framework, reaffirming the company’s near- and long-term growth expectations. The investigation could last for up to 18 months, which I believe makes the EIX stock an attractive investment to profit from the company’s industry-leading dividend policy, at least over the next 12-month horizon. What’s more, the downside from the Eaton fire could turn into an upside thereafter, as the situation around the blaze only necessitates the need for incremental grid investment, providing a strong regulatory backdrop for robust rate base growth.

data.title: Costco Stock: World-Class Company, Stretched Valuation--Patience Is Key
data.description:
Here’s where my head’s at: Costco (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:COST) is a phenomenal company and frankly, I wish every stock on my watchlist had this kind of balance sheet and brand loyalty. But at over $1,000 a share, right near all-time highs, I just can’t convince myself that piling in at today’s price is smart risk/reward.

I don’t think there’s anything fundamentally “wrong” with Costco. The business is humming, comps are up, members keep coming back and their moat is as wide as ever. The issue, at least for me, is price. You’re paying more than 55 times next year’s earnings for mid-to-high single digit growth. That’s a serious stretch even for a “quality compounder” like Costco. I’m not bearish but I also can’t call it a Buy here. This is a classic Hold.

If you already own it, I wouldn’t be selling—just maybe dial back your expectations for near-term upside from here. If you’re on the outside looking in, I’d keep this one on the watchlist and hope for some market turbulence to bring it back to earth.

Costco’s Simple Model, Big Results

Costco’s business model is almost comically simple but it works: charge a membership fee, sell high-quality stuff at razor-thin margins and move mountains of product every day. It’s all about scale and customer trust. They’re up to 905 warehouses now, 624 in the U.S. alone and are still expanding internationally with China and other newer markets getting more attention.

The real secret sauce is the membership engine. Those fees go straight to the bottom line and renewal rates keep hovering around 90% globally. Kirkland (their private label) has quietly become an absolute beast. For context: Kirkland’s sales alone are bigger than Lowe’s or P&G. Not many retailers can say their in-house brand is an S&P 500 rival in its own right.

Management isn’t sitting still either. They’ve ramped up digital investments, partnered with Affirm to bring more flexible payment options to their online store and just approved another 27 new warehouse openings for 2025. They also keep bumping up the dividend—up 12% just this spring.

Industry and Market Landscape

Costco basically owns the warehouse club space. Sam’s Club and BJ’s are fine but they aren’t breathing down Costco’s neck. Broader retail is always cutthroat but Costco’s value proposition really shines when times get tough. People want bargains and private label is having a moment thanks to inflation and cost-conscious consumers.

That said, there are some shifting winds. Comp sales are still up (6% in May) but that’s actually a deceleration from the previous run-rate. You also have regulatory stuff on the horizon (tariffs, labor lawsuits, privacy) and while none of it seems existential it could be a distraction or a drag at some point. China’s an opportunity but also a wild card.

Financial Analysis

The numbers are rock solid. Year-to-date sales: $189B up 8%. Net income’s up about 9.5%. Margins? They’re squeezing out a little more (TTM gross margin at 12.8%) and TTM EBIT margin is stable at 3.75%. Free cash flow is up double-digits and the cash pile is huge—almost $15B versus $8B in debt. Return on equity is a wild 32%. No liquidity issues here.

The payout ratio on the dividend is under 30%. They’re buying back some stock but not aggressively. I see this as prudent capital management rather than some desperate attempt to boost EPS. All things considered this is one of the cleanest balance sheets you’ll find in retail.

Valuation Analysis

Here’s where the math gets tough for me. The P/E is 57x trailing, 55x forward. EV/EBITDA? Over 35x. You’re paying about 16.6x book value. Even with generous growth assumptions, say high-single-digit earnings growth for the next few years—you just don’t get much upside. The PEG ratio is north of 6. That’s nosebleed territory.

I ran a DCF analysis using fairly generous assumptions (discount rate 7%, long-term growth around 8%) and it put fair value right around $900. That’s not catastrophic but it means you’re paying up for zero margin of safety.

Honestly you could see a 10-15% drop just on multiple compression—even if the business keeps executing.

Recent News Sentiment & Market Perception

Everybody loves Costco right now. The headlines are universally positive—beat-and-raise quarters outpacing Walmart and Target, Kirkland flexing its muscles, digital initiatives getting buzz. But with this much love comes sky-high expectations. Even after a solid earnings beat ($4.28 EPS, $63.2B revenue) analysts estimate revisions are basically flat. And I’ve noticed an uptick in short interest in consumer staples—maybe just a hedge but maybe some folks are starting to doubt how much further this can run.

Bottom line: any disappointment, even a small one could hit the stock harder than people think.

What Could Go Wrong

Here’s what would keep me up at night if I were loading up on shares here:

  • Valuation getting reset: If the market falls out of love with “quality at any price” Costco’s multiple could drop 20-30% fast.
  • Slowing comps: We’re already seeing a bit of this. If comps settle in the low single digits I doubt the market will keep rewarding it with a 50x+ multiple.
  • Legal and regulatory: There’s a lot of litigation and privacy regulation noise especially in California and on the data side.
  • International expansion not going as planned: New geographies are tricky and missteps (especially in China) could get expensive.
  • Consumer slowdown: If the macro really rolls over even Costco will feel it.

I know the bulls say Costco is “different” and deserves the premium. Maybe. But it’s not immune to gravity forever.

Forward-Looking Outlook

Over the next year or two, I see Costco plugging away, mid-to-high single-digit revenue and earnings growth, high renewal rates, maybe some incremental wins from e-commerce and pharmacy. Wall Street is modeling $18 EPS for 2025. Long term, if the international bet pays off and digital initiatives stick, they’ll keep compounding—just maybe not at the breakneck pace the multiple assumes.

Bear, Base and Bull Scenarios

Bear case ($750): Valuation compresses, comps slow and we get a correction. Down 25% wouldn’t shock me if sentiment really turns.

Base case ($900): Costco keeps on keeping on—solid growth, strong renewals but the market re-rates the multiple. Shares drift sideways or down a bit from here.

Bull case ($1100): Execution is flawless, international takes off, digital pays dividends and the market shrugs off valuation. Maybe you squeeze out 10% upside but I wouldn’t bet on it at this entry.

Wrapping Up: Why I’m Not a Buyer Here

So to sum up where I stand: I think Costco is one of the best businesses out there but the stock’s just too expensive for me right now. If you already own it, hold. But if you’re thinking about starting a position I’d wait. If Costco pulls back to the $850–$900 range I’d get a lot more interested and think the risk-reward would start to look more attractive.

If you’re a long-term holder, great—just don’t expect a rocket ride from here. If you’re new money, patience is your friend. Costco isn’t going anywhere. I’d just rather buy it when the story is a little less popular and the price gives you some breathing room.

data.title: Dynex Capital: 16% Yield With Some Discipline Behind It
data.description:
Introduction

When I looked at Dynex Capital (NYSE:DX), the first thing I saw was, of course, this insane, over 16% dividend yield. That immediately makes you curious about what the problem is with this stock, because, most likely, there is one. The main reason to worry comes from the type of business it’s in. It’s a high-yielding mREIT (mortgage REIT), and they can be black holes for capital if you don’t understand how that yield is being generated, what risks you’re taking on, and whether it’s even sustainable. But I am on a mission to find the best dividend stocks, so I decided to dive deep into this company. I listened to the call, read the filings, looked at everything I could find, like the hedging, the leverage, the portfolio mix, and honestly, this is one of the few mREITs that looks like it actually earns the money it pays out and is not slowly dying.

Company Overview

I would like to start with a very quick overview of the company, its business, and the latest results. The main difference between REIT and Dynex (or mREIT) is that it doesn’t own real estate. It doesn’t rent out apartments or collect rent from tenants. Instead, it buys mortgage-backed securities, mostly agency RMBS (Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities). When you buy RMBS, you’re basically investing in the cash flow from a bunch of homeowners making their mortgage payments. If they pay on time, you get steady income. If they don’t - well, that’s the risk. But when they’re agency RMBS (the kind Dynex invests in), they’re backed by Fannie or Freddie, so you don’t take on the credit risk of homeowners defaulting. You’re mostly just dealing with interest rate risk. These are the safest type of mortgage bonds from a credit perspective, because the government’s backing reduces default risk to almost zero. There are some unknowns about Fannie and Freddie right now, but I will mention it a little later.

So basically, the company finances the purchases with short-term borrowings (usually through repos), and the spread between the yield on the MBS and the cost of borrowing is their profit. It sounds simple, but keeping that spread steady, hedging rate risk, and managing leverage in volatile environments is not easy, and this is why it’s a pretty risky type of investment, although I must say, Dynex looks like they know what they are doing.

In Q1, DX posted a 2.6% total economic return, which includes the dividend and the book value change. Book value dropped slightly from $12.70 to $12.56, which, considering the macro backdrop and the moves in Treasury and swap markets, is not bad.

Core earnings (or “earnings available for distribution”) came in at $0.21 per share, well below the $0.47 in dividends paid for the quarter. That gap isn’t ideal, but management seems comfortable using prior gains and retained cash to maintain the payout, for now. They’re clearly betting that spreads will remain attractive enough to make up for the shortfall over time. Still, it’s worth flagging that the dividend wasn’t technically covered by earnings this quarter, even if they have the capital to support it in the near term.

GAAP net income was slightly negative, mostly due to mark-to-market hedge movements, not actual cash losses. So while reported earnings look weak, the company still generated enough cash from spread income and portfolio moves to cover the dividend without having to borrow or liquidate anything aggressively. They’re not exactly “stretching” in a reckless sense, but they are relying on spread tailwinds and strong capital positioning to keep the payout going.

Portfolio Construction

Their portfolio is simple and clean. About 98% of their $11.1 billion portfolio is in agency RMBS, with a mix of specified pools and TBA positions (agreements to buy or sell mortgage bonds in the future). Most of it is in 4.5% to 5.5% coupon bonds, the part of the stack that still offers solid yield and stable prepayment behavior.

By the way, Dynex is not giving up about the too quick prepayments. After the Rocket and Mr. Cooper merger, there has been talk that we could see a new wave of refinancing in certain parts of the market. So Dynex quickly shifted into more stable pools of borrowers who already have low mortgage rates and don’t really have a reason to refinance. It’s a simple way to protect the income stream, but an effective one. I like that they are actively managing it, always a good sign. They also added a small piece of agency CMBS and some interest-only strips, but that doesn’t change the overall picture. The portfolio is still clean, liquid, and mostly made up of high-quality agency RMBS.

Leverage, Liquidity, Hedging

Leverage was 7.4x at quarter-end, and while it went up slightly post-quarter due to book value changes, it’s still in a comfortable range. They have around $790 million in liquidity (cash and unencumbered assets), which gives them room to deal with margin calls or with sudden market pressures.

What I really liked was how they raised $270 million in capital during the quarter, and did it above book value. It means they took existing shareholders into account and didn’t dilute anyone. About two-thirds of this money was deployed into the agency RMBS market, and the rest was kept, which is a smart move. This is a quote from the earnings call:

Year-to-date, we've raised $270 million of new capital, and given the strength of our stock during the first quarter, we raised capital at a premium to book value, which is accretive to shareholders.

Dynex’s hedge book is mostly made up of interest rate swaps, which makes a lot more sense for mortgage exposure than just using Treasury futures. Most of those swaps are focused on the long end of the curve, so if long-term rates start rising faster than short-term ones, like in a steepening scenario, they’re covered. Basically, they’re just making sure they won’t get caught off guard if conditions change fast. It’s a pretty cautious setup, but a justified one.

The good news is that it seems to be working. For the last five quarters, the hedge benefit was around $25 million each quarter consistently. You can see it directly in how their net interest income turned around from a $3 million loss last year to $28 million this quarter.

Macro

This isn’t an easy market, and Dynex knows it. They’re not pretending things are about to stabilize, because, objectively, it doesn't look so. There are too many moving parts, and it’s impossible to predict where the wind will go next. The company is right in the middle of everything that is happening - trade policies, Fed uncertainty, global capital shifts, and increasing noise around potential changes to Fannie and Freddie.

But that’s exactly the kind of setup this portfolio was built for. Volatility like this tends to drive spreads higher, especially in agency RMBS, and that’s where they see value. They’re not taking on more risk just because yields look tempting. They’ve kept leverage reasonable and held a good amount of liquidity. If things stay messy but manageable, or if rates start to settle, they’re positioned to keep earning and maybe even recover some book value in the process.

Another important piece of news is everything that is happening around Fannie and Freddie. There is a talk about taking it out of conservatorship, and if that ever turns into real policy action, it could hit the agency mortgage market hard. Dynex seems to be watching it closely, but still, it isn’t something the company can control.

Something positive to not end on a bad note: since their IPO, Dynex has delivered a total return of 477.6% with dividends reinvested. It means that in a span of around 32 years, the stock returned approximately 7.5% annually on average.

Now, obviously, that return includes some wild cycles, since they IPOed in 1992. Different rate regimes, the 2008 crisis, COVID, everything. But what it shows is that this isn’t a company that just recently figured out how to manage interest rate risk. They’ve been doing it through every kind of market environment. The stock is down in the long run, as it usually is with mREITs, but as you can see, the compound was worth the time of the investors.

Conclusion

I’m not saying this is risk-free. It’s an mREIT, and those come with plenty of things to watch. But for what it is, Dynex looks solid. The yield is high, the balance sheet is clean, and they’re not playing games with leverage or chasing risky assets just to boost numbers. If you’re okay with some rate sensitivity and you’re building a dividend portfolio that can handle a bit of movement, this one could actually work. Just don’t forget to check in on it from time to time. If you want to set your portfolio and forget about it, I suggest going for lower risk and, as a result, lower yield.

data.title: Voyager's IPO Rocketed, But Here's Why Investors Should Brace For Impact
data.description:
Voyager Technologies (NYSE:VOYG) had a splashy IPO with shares appreciating by 82% on their first trading day. This follows an upsized IPO, entering the market at $31/share with a 12.3mm share issuance, raising $383mm. Despite the initial excitement in the equities market as well as the long-term contract potential for the DoD and space organizations, I do not believe the growth potential supports a 16.25x price/sales multiple. For this reason, I am recommending VOYG shares with a SELL rating with a price target of $33.49/share.

Voyager Technologies Operations

Voyager had roughly a 50/50 split between Defense & National Security and Space Solutions. In q1’25, the percentage from D&NS increased to 66.1%, in line with contracts materializing for the organization.

Voyager works with over 20 government space and defense agencies as well as commercial organizations. For public agencies, Voyager supports NASA, various components of the DoD, including the MDA, the US Air Force, and the US Space Force, as well as a variety of international space organizations. In commercial, Voyager works with a variety of defense contractors, including Lockheed Martin (LMT), Northrop Grumman (NOC), RTX Corporation (RTX), as well as space companies like Blue Origin and Maxar.

In 2024, Voyager was selected by Lockheed Martin as a sub-contractor to deliver the upper stage propulsion subsystem and optical guidance technology for the Missile Defense Agency’s [MDA] Next Generation Interceptor [NGI] program. The NGI program is designed to defend against ballistic missile threats from rogue nations as a first line of defense.

Voyager’s optical guidance technology is also leveraged by RTX Corporation (RTX) and Millenium (BA) in their respective missile defense initiatives and small satellite constellation programs.

Accordingly, Voyager’s solid propulsion technology has the capabilities to provide the level of thrust required for hypersonic applications. As of the release of the S-1, Voyager had $78mm in contract value to provide an advanced solid-propulsion subsystem for Lockheed Martin’s NGI contract with the MDA. This contract has the potential to advance Voyager’s positioning in further developments in missile defense interceptors and kill vehicles, strategic, tactical, and hypersonic missiles, and reentry systems.

As part of its geolocation technologies, Voyager integrates its proprietary solutions with Palantir’s (PLTR) operating system to create advanced, AI-enabled analytics for national security agencies and defense primes.

As for growth in DoD work, space defense may be a new horizon that Voyager can benefit from. With its expertise in propulsion systems, Voyager may be leveraged as part of the proposed Golden Dome project. If pushed through, this could be a major initiative, providing significant revenue streams for space defense companies. Given the depth of the project, I suspect that funding will be spread across multiple vendors with significant overlap. For example, Intuitive Machines (LUNR) is developing a nuclear-powered propulsion system for stealth satellites with the US Air Force.

President Trump is currently seeking to allocate $25b to launch the initiative, which some government officials expect to cost a total of $175b and be completed within a 3-year timeframe. Others anticipate that the project could cost as much as $542b and take over 20 years to develop and launch.

The DoD budget request for fiscal year 2025 was $850b of which $11b may fall into Voyager’s addressable market for research and development, test, and evaluation. As for the hypersonic missile defense system, the 2025 DoD budget includes $2b that can be addressed by Voyager.

Through its acquisitions, Voyager has been a part of over 1,000 missions and has supplied over 220 spacecraft subsystems and 330 satellites for commercial and government programs. In 2020, Voyager developed the Bishop Airlock that was permanently attached to the International Space Station [ISS] to allow payloads to enter the facility.

Subsequently, Voyager is currently in the development phase of the Starlab Space Station, the replacement for the ISS, which is expected to be decommissioned in 2030. Accordingly, Voyager completed Phase 1 in the initial design work for NASA, receiving funding to undergo Phase 2 hardware development. As of q1’25, Voyager had $70.3mm of the $217.5mm in funding remaining.

The Starlab business is operated as a joint venture between Voyager at 67% ownership interest, Airbus at 30.5%, and Mitsubishi, MDA Space, and Palantir each with under 1%. The Starlab is expected to have a total value of $16b, $1b of which Voyager serves. In total, management anticipates Starlab to cost in the range of $2.80-3.3b to manufacture and launch. As of q1’25, Voyager has already secured a launch contract with SpaceX for its Starship launch vehicle for 2029 and is expected to deliver Starlab as one component.

Airbus will provide a significant proportion of the heavy lifting, including technical design and engineering services, as well as the construction of the habitat. MDA Space will contribute an assortment of robotics to operate the mission. Palantir’s digital twins and AI technologies will be utilized to enhance operations in the facility.

Once operational, Starlab is expected to be offered as a service for a variety of different industries for pharmaceutical development or advanced semiconductor fabrication. Utilization of the Starlab is expected to be offered as a service, allowing for Voyager and its partners to generate recurring revenue from its operations. Management plans on raising capital for the development of Starlab through prebuys, essentially allowing for customers to reserve their slot for operations aboard the space facility. Given the design, Starlab can be expanded with new components, allowing for the facility to scale overtime and mitigate the risk of overbuilding up front.

Voyager Technologies Financial Position

Model Note: loss from operations for periods not explicitly stated in the S-1 are inferred from EBITDA net of depreciation & amortization.

Looking at Voyager’s financials, revenue growth was relatively low in FY24, growing at a mere 6% despite Voyager being a part of significant space and defense initiatives. Despite the historical performance, the outlook appears robust given the strong potential for defense contracts for its hypersonic propulsion technology as well as the commercial space station, though these projects may be years out from materializing into high-growth potentials.

Margins have largely been loss-generating and may continue to remain negative as the organization gears up for potentially high-volume defense contracts. Looking out long-term, I believe Voyager may become adjusted EBITDA-positive by eFY28 while ramping up margins in eFY29 as the Starlab gears up for commercialization.

Risks Related to Voyager Technologies

Bull Case

Voyager’s technology may play a major role in the next generation of national defense. With foreign actors possessing hypersonic missiles, the need to have a satellite intercept system may be vital to ensure the appropriate protection for the US, driving the need for the Golden Dome initiative. Along with developing technology that may align with this project, Voyager has its sights set on the Starlab as a commercial space station that may provide long-term recurring revenue for the organization.

Bear Case

Voyager may be considered a long-duration investment as the Starlab isn’t set for launch until 2029. With companies like Redwire (RDW) and Intuitive Machines (LUNR) developing pharmaceutical kits for development in space, the opportunity for Starlab may not be as robust as anticipated as these missions may not require a permanent satellite fixture.

In addition to this, funding for the Golden Dome project is awaiting approval and potentially risks not going through. Following the IPO, VOYG shares realized a significant increase, skyrocketing to 16.25x price/sales, adding certain risks of crashing back to Earth.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Given that Voyager long-duration is a long-duration investment, I believe it will be best to value shares using a discount cash flow [DCF] model. Using the financial model presented in the Voyager Technologies Financial Position section above, the current share price reflects a significant premium, even when discounted out to eFY29.

When compared to a close peer, Intuitive Machines, LUNR shares trade at 5.53x price/sales; at this premium, VOYG shares should be trading at $19.18/share based on the valuation model above. Given the relatively high valuation, I am rating VOYG shares with a SELL rating with a price target of $33.49/share at a 7.50% discount rate with a terminal growth rate of 5.55%.

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.

data.title: Krispy Kreme's Growth Story Crumbles
data.description:
Krispy Kreme (NASDAQ:DNUT) shares have cratered more than 74% in the past 12 months, and the company withdrew its full-year guidance after reporting weak Q1 results and acknowledging softness in both revenue and demand across the U.S. Furthermore, The McDonald’s (MCD) partnership that once billed as a game changer for the donut maker is now being reevaluated. If we add the consumer sentiment, which has declined to its second-lowest reading on record, and Krispy Kreme underperforming nearly every major QSR stock, I’m turning more cautious. Although the long-term vision might still look attainable, the near-term execution risk looks far too high.

What We Hoped for and What We Got Instead

I covered Krispy Kreme following its Q3 results, at a time when investor attention was focused on the nationwide rollout with McDonald’s. At that point, the company had just announced a massive access point expansion plan, with hopes of reaching 23,000 access points in the U.S. over the next two years, up from around 8,000 at the time. Management was also projecting material improvements in hub utilization, margin expansion, and long-term EPS growth from $0.20 in 2024 to a projected $0.54 by 2026.

The narrative then was simple: McDonald’s scale would reignite growth and unlock operating leverage. Hubs like the one in Elk Grove, Chicago, were suddenly running triple shifts, and the firm claimed access point CAGR would rise from 19% to 33% in the next couple of years. Analysts were projecting a massive expansion in both revenue and EBITDA.

Fast forward to today, and the optimism has faded.

Demand Weakness Shows Across the Board

Net revenue in Q1 came in at $375.2 million, a decline of $67.5 million due to the divestiture of the majority stake in Insomnia Cookies in Q3 of fiscal 2024. However, it was still at the lower end of the company's prior guidance. Organic revenue declined by 1%, dragged down by softness in the U.S. segment and a continued reduction in discount days as part of the firm’s margin-focused strategy. Unsurprisingly, Adjusted EBITDA took a massive hit, falling to just $24 million, translating to a margin of only 6.4%, compared to $58.2 million and 13.1% last year.

The U.S. segment saw organic revenue decline by 2.6%, as new access points, which include over 2,400 McDonald’s locations, actually failed to generate the volume needed to offset the softer traffic. According to management, after the initial launch, the demand has dropped below what the firm had hoped for. Average sales per door per week in the U.S. fell to $587, down from last year and below the levels seen prior to the McDonald’s partnership.

Meanwhile, international equity-owned markets delivered 1.5% organic growth, with Canada and Australia offering a few bright spots. However, EBITDA margins in the segment also fell by 200 basis points to 12.5%, and management admitted to ongoing inefficiencies in U.K. retail operations.

In the franchise-heavy Market Development segment, organic growth came in at 2.7%, but EBITDA was down 7.2%, weighed down by recent acquisitions now reflected in U.S. results. However, both the International and Market Development and International segments represent barely 37% of all revenue; therefore, their minor growth was unable to offset the bigger slump in US sales.

The company also posted a negative $0.05 in adjusted EPS and burned $20.8 million in operating cash flow, impacted by ongoing inefficiencies related to the prior cybersecurity incident.

Yet, we did see management reiterating their plans to return to positive operating cash flow in 2025, but right now, the near-term road looks rather murky.

McDonald’s Isn’t the Silver Bullet

The most disappointing development in Krispy Kreme’s recent update was the stall in its much-hyped McDonald’s rollout. I had huge expectations that partnering with "Golden Arches" would bring some great access points at locations with strong footfall and a clear intention among customers to treat themselves.

The initial launch showed promising results, with strong marketing support and encouraging early traction. But that momentum didn’t last long as sales at McDonald’s locations quickly fell below expectations. In response, the company has paused its Q2 expansion plans and is working with McDonald’s to reassess the broader deployment strategy.

While this may appear to be a sudden pivot, the underlying softness of the U.S market has been building since early 2024. In Q1 of the last fiscal year, average weekly revenue per door in the U.S., which is Krispy Kreme’s largest market, responsible for about 63% of total sales, started to fall. At the time, the change seemed marginal, especially in the context of rapid access point growth. But more recent figures show a sharper and more persistent decline in sales, as seen in the image I referenced earlier. Average revenue per door per week has dropped by 8.3% from $640 to $587.

For me, the bigger concern is what this signals strategically. McDonald’s was seen as Krispy Kreme’s last major lever to reignite growth after multiple quarters of deceleration. With that lever now in question, the tone inside the company has clearly shifted.

Instead of accelerating, Krispy Kreme is retrenching. It’s closing underperforming access points, outsourcing logistics, and aiming to fully transition to third-party delivery in the U.S. by mid-2026. Management expects 15% to have been outscored by the end of May. That may eventually help margins stabilize, but it also underscores how inefficient the current operating model must have become.

The focus of Krispy Kreme now is less about expansion and more about survival. Management has pivoted toward restoring positive free cash flow, cutting debt, and shedding unprofitable operations. The company is entering a phase of financial discipline with less focus on growth. This ultimately puts at risk their ambition to scale to 23,000 access points on U.S. soil by the end of 2026.

However, not everything was bleak in the last report. Early feedback from new partnerships with Costco (COST) and Sam’s Club has been promising, and Krispy Kreme is experimenting with secondary displays at retailers like Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and Kroger (KR) to drive impulse buys. But these are still in their early stages and unlikely to make up for the projected lost volume from the scaled-back McDonald’s initiative anytime soon. Reflecting this shift, the company had no other option but to withdraw its full-year guidance.

The original forecast called for net revenue between $1.55 billion and $1.65 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $180 million to $200 million. Based on what we’ve seen so far and management's estimates for Q2, revenue may still fall within that range, with the analysts' consensus currently standing at $1.55 billion. But I believe EBITDA could come in lower, likely between $140 million and $150 million.

At this point, Krispy Kreme appears to lack a credible path to reignite meaningful growth. The ambitious projections floated last summer now look increasingly out of reach. The core issue remains the same: generating consistent demand at scale. And based on both management’s updated tone and the market’s muted reaction, that missing piece is still nowhere in sight.

Valuation That Reflects the Uncertainty

On the surface, Krispy Kreme shares already look beaten down enough to attract contrarian interest. The stock is down more than 70% year-to-date and around 78% in the past three years, making it by far the worst performer among quick-service restaurant peers. And this does not mean that there have been some shocking changes in the consumer environment, rather the opposite.

For comparison, Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) is up 88% in the last 3 years, McDonald’s has gained nearly 27%, and even Starbucks, despite its recent struggles, has posted a positive 20% return. Meanwhile, Shake Shack (SHAK) is in a league of its own, up 192% for the same period.

Despite the dramatic price reset, Krispy Kreme’s valuation looks attractive, but it still doesn’t scream opportunity. Its EV/EBITDA stands at 13.6x forward earnings, which is below peers like Cheesecake Factory (15.3x) and McDonald’s (18.7x). However, let's not forget that those are businesses with consistent profitability and stable free cash flow. Something Krispy Kreme cannot show off with.

Its Price/Sales ratio of just 0.31 also looks attractive at first glance, but I think it is a reflection of the market's huge skepticism rather than an undervalued gem. Investors are already pricing in execution risk, debt burden, and the absence of credible near-term growth.

And that skepticism seems justified. While the average price target sits around still sits way high at $8.05, suggesting 174% upside potential from current levels, I don't think that this target truly reflects the challenges the business has faced and might soon be corrected further downward.

One potential lifeline could come from international business. Management has floated the idea of refranchising select overseas markets, which could bring in much-needed cash. Besides, the company has also just announced that it received the last tranche of the Insomnia Cookie sale, adding another $75 million to the balance sheet. If executed wisely, that capital could go toward paying down debt and funding strategic CapEx, buying Krispy Kreme time to rebuild its domestic momentum.

However, with the McDonald’s partnership stalled, full-year guidance withdrawn, and the company pivoting to cash preservation over expansion, there are too many unknowns to justify chasing this name, even before we account for all the risks.

Risks Are Mounting, and Consumer Weakness Isn’t Helping

The company’s decision to halt its dividend and slow down expansion efforts is prudent, but it also signals a company that is no longer in growth mode, which will ultimately put their ambition to have 23,000 access points in the U.S. by the end of 2026 at huge risk.

Of course, part of that decision has to do with the deterioration of the broader macro picture. The U.S. consumer sentiment has declined to its second-lowest reading on record. This weakness is already felt in the QSR industry, with major players such as McDonald’s citing weakness among low-income consumers and families.

Commodity costs could also play a major role, especially the cacao, which remains a wildcard given the potential tariff change and is already trading at near all-time highs.

Last but not least comes the pure operational risk. As the firm trims doors, restructures leadership, and outsources logistics, execution will play a critical role.

The Bull Case Has Thinned Out

Just three months ago, it looked like Krispy Kreme was on the point of unlocking a powerful catalyst for growth through McDonald’s and more efficient distribution. Now, after the company has retracted its guidance, operating margins feeling the pressure, and the McDonald’s rollout is going to be reviewed, the risk-reward has shifted dramatically.

In my opinion, shares may have priced in the narrative shift, but they haven’t priced in the possibility that the business could remain stuck in low gear for the next couple of years. Without a credible growth engine, Krispy Kreme’s valuation looks like a value trap, not a bargain at this point. For now, the risk-reward profile simply doesn’t justify investing in the business.

data.title: Taking A Speculative Position In Victoria's Secret
data.description:
A little over two years ago, I decided to buy some Victoria’s Secret & Co. (NYSE:VSCO), and since then, the shares are basically flat. Although I didn’t write about it on this platform, I actually sold my shares last November for a pretty decent 67% gain. The shares have dropped in price since then, though, which has put them back on my radar. I want to determine whether or not it makes sense to buy again. I’ll make that determination by looking at the financial results since I last wrote about the name, and by reviewing the valuation.

Note On the BBRC Drama

Before getting into the analysis, I need to comment briefly on BBRC’s attempt to take over the company. You can read about the drama here and here if you’re interested in such things. I believe management will deploy effective countermeasures against this attempt, and BBRC’s control interest will remain effectively stuck at 13%. This means that the analysis below assumes a level of continuity going forward. My views on this may change as the drama unfolds, but for the moment, the conflict between The Board and BBRC is not that relevant to my decision to buy or hold at current prices.

Welcome to the “thesis statement” portion of our programming. This is where I offer my readers the “highlight reel” of my article, so they can avoid all of the terrible jokes and proper spelling. You’re welcome. The financial performance here has been mediocre at best. The latest quarterly results were rescued by a reduction in the interest expense. I remain of the view that it would have been better for the company to pay down debt than to initiate a buyback, but that’s the past, and it’s a past that doesn’t impact me anymore, so it’s hard to remain too salty about it. I think the shares are now unreasonably cheap, and for that reason, I’ll be happy to buy back into the stock. Because the company doesn’t spin off income, this is a speculative buy. I’ll pick up 300 shares and may add to my position depending on how the future unfolds.

Financial Snapshot

In some ways, the most recent quarter was soft relative to the same period a year ago. For example, revenue was off by about ½ a percent, while gross profit dropped by about 5.4%, as the company failed to squeeze costs out of the system. On the plus side, earnings before taxes spiked by about $790 thousand because interest expense was about $4.64 million lower than the same period a year ago. So, the operating company was about 5% less profitable, but earnings were “less bad” because interest expenses were significantly lower than in the same period last year.

Zooming out a bit and reviewing the financial history of the company from 2021 to the present, things look pretty awful in my view. Specifically, revenue and net income in 2024 were about 8.2%, and 74% (!) lower than they were in 2021. You may remember from history class that the world was recovering from a global pandemic back then, and the idea that 2025 was worse than that era is off-putting in my view.

Given the above, the shares would need to be very cheap indeed for me to consider holding them.

The Stock

I've written it before, and I'm absolutely certain that I'll write it again. I may risk boring my readers, but it must be obvious to them that that’s a risk I’m very willing to take. Here goes, one more time. The more you pay for $1 of future gains, the lower will be your subsequent returns. This is why I try my best to buy shares when they are cheaply priced. Put another way, there’s a strongly negative relationship between the price paid and future returns. Said slightly differently, “buy low, sell high.” Obviously, the only way to “buy low” is when the crowd is willing to sell you a stock at a cheap price. They’re only ever going to be willing to do that if they don’t see value in the shares. They’ll only ever fail to see value in the shares if the company the shares supposedly represent has “hair” on it.

I write this incessantly because I’ve frequently come across people who are of the view that "we don't buy stocks, we buy businesses." In my view, these poor souls have been fed some propaganda. I’m going to do my bit to try to dispel this nonsense by offering a thought experiment. Let’s consider the tale of two investors who bought Victoria’s Secret at 2 different times. One of them was bought on August 2nd of last year at a price of about $16.20. Another bought exactly four months later, on December 2nd at $41.90. Before I go on, I’ll point out that December 2nd happens to be my birthday. Don’t worry, I’ll send out gift ideas about a month beforehand this year. Anyway, the investor who bought in August is up about 35% at the moment. The person who bought virtually identical shares only 4 months later has lost about 48% of their capital on the shares. The company didn’t change sufficiently over those four months to justify this radical difference in returns, in my view. You buy the stock, and you’re more often than not rewarded when you buy it cheaply and more often than not punished when you buy it when it’s dear.

With that sermonizing out of the way, I should point out that I measure the cheapness of a stock in a few ways, ranging from the simple to the more complex. On the simple side, I like to look at ratios of price to some measure of economic value, like earnings, free cash, book value, and the like. I like to see shares trading at a discount to both their own history and the overall market. When I last reviewed Victoria’s Secret, I got excited by the fact that the market was paying only $.29 for $1 of sales. The shares are about as cheap today. The market is also only paying about 11 times earnings, which is quite inexpensive in my view.

On the more complex side, I want to move past ratios and try to understand what the market is currently "assuming" about the future of a given company. If the crowd is assuming great things from the company, that's a sign that the shares are generally expensive. If you read my articles regularly, you know that I rely on the work of Professor Stephen Penman and his book "Accounting for Value" for this. In this book, Penman walks investors through how they can apply the magic of high school algebra to a standard finance formula in order to work out what the market is "thinking" about a given company's future growth. This involves isolating the "g" (growth) variable in this formula. I like this approach because it uses the stock price itself as a source of information, and then infers what the market is currently "expecting" about the future.

Anyway, applying this approach to Victoria’s Secret at the moment suggests the market is assuming that this company will now grow profits at a rate of about 1.4% from here, which is nicely pessimistic in my view. Given the above, I’m willing to buy the shares once more. The ride will be a rough one, and I don’t expect the shares to go “gangbusters” as the young people say, but I don’t think we’re harmed buying sustainable businesses at cheap prices.

data.title: Investing And AI: What May Be Ahead
data.description:
What may lay ahead for the development of artificial intelligence and how can investors potentially benefit? MoneyTalk discusses with Evan Chen, VP, Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - One of the big of investing themes of the past couple of years has been the rapid development of artificial intelligence. But where are we in that evolution, and what does it mean for investors?

Joining us now to discuss, Evan Chen, VP of Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management. Great to have you on the program. Great topic to queue in here today.

Evan Chen - Thank you. I bet you never hear about it.

Greg Bonnell - Never hear about Al. But you know what? I mean, we hear a lot about it because there's something happening here, it's probably going to be very important for all of our lives and for our investing lives. So let's start with a bit of an update on the AI universe, how it's shaping market returns, what's been working. And I think you have some pictures to draw for us.

Evan Chen - Yeah, so I have a framework and my contribution to some of these BBB things that are coming out, the Big Beautiful Bill. Well, I have the Boom, Build, and Benefit framework. Before we launch into that, I want to draw a quick picture as a way to ground ourselves here.

Greg Bonnell - All right, so we're going to see-- we're going to telestrate on the show. This is what we call this. We call it telestrator when you draw over maps.

Evan Chen - I'm no artist, so you have to bear with me here.

Greg Bonnell - We got a line that's going up. Usually, that's good.

Evan Chen - There we go.

Greg Bonnell - We got a line going down. I'll reserve judgment on the line going down. Another line going down.

Evan Chen - So this is what we call an S-curve. It's something that analysts have used for years now to describe life cycles of new technologies. So this first part of the curve, the left part, is what we call the initial growth phase. So this is a new technology. Call it AI. Call it the internet. Call it rails. Call it phone lines, whatever.

It's a slow ascent up because the market's not quite there yet. Maybe the technology's not there yet. There's barriers to market adoption. But as we get to a critical mass, we hit the second point. This is the S-curve. This is what we call rapid growth. And this is where the technology is used more and more often. It becomes standardized across industries, across workplaces, across daily life, and rapid, rapid growth.

Then finally, we get to the plateau, where we reach market saturation. There's really no more room to grow here. And it grows in line with basically GDP. So I think a lot of people would argue that we're in the second part of the growth, so the rapid growth phase. There's a lot of new websites out there, apps that use AI. But I would argue we're more in this initial growth phase still. And I want to go through that framework with you.

Greg Bonnell - OK, so let's go through that growth phase, because I think that's a big question for people. Are we in the chip phase? Are we in the-- it starts to become a part of my daily lives. And I think you're saying we're getting to that next phase there. So walk us through all the parts.

Evan Chen - Yeah, so the first phase, what I call boom, and boom is the critical mass of chips needed to actually make AI models like ChatGPT. So I like to think of chips as the heart of AI, constantly churning out calculations used to power the models.

If we look at chips-- Nvidia (NVDA), I'm sure everyone knows. The stock has been up 900% in the past two years, with it dragging up the entire semis index up 90% over the same time frame. But we've talked about enough about Nvidia. I want to explore what's next.

Greg Bonnell - So what is next? If the chips are the first thing, the heart of what's going on here, where do we take this?

Evan Chen - So the second is what I call the build phase. So the build is the $700 billion of CapEx that's going into data centers. So it's being built by the hyperscalers, so that's Microsoft, Amazon, and Google. And then there's also some neoclouds, GPU clouds, Nebeus and CoreWeave.

So I like to think of the data centers and the build infrastructure layer as a nervous system of AI. So it's translating that heartbeat into where it needs to go. Data centers were initially used in the cloud and the internet days to connect everything. And they're used the same as AI, connecting the compute to where it needs to go to power some of these models.

So we know that there's a lot of demand. All the hyperscalers-- Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOGL)-- have all said that there's too much demand for the supply. So where is that extra demand going? Well, we've seen it run up in the stocks to the Neoclouds, Nebeus and CoreWeave (CRWV), up 200%, 50% on the year, as there's just too much demand here.

So I think this area is just getting started. I think it's just inflecting up in that S-curve. So if we look at the curve, chips were infinitesimally a small part. And I think we're moving to the beginning of this area here with the cloud.

Greg Bonnell - That's interesting. So if the boom-- the chips are just a small part of all this, and the build, the cloud, the infrastructure's starting to take hold, the benefits. I think a lot of people have been asking those questions. I've seen the run up in the chips. We're hearing about AI all over the place. So how is that going to affect my daily life? Where is it going to start permeating through society?

Evan Chen - Yeah, benefits where we get to the good stuff, get that S-curve going. So benefit refers to applications or the software layer. So I like to think of the benefit or the software as the voice of AI. So all the stuff is in the back end and the data centers and the chips. But for it to interact with the real world, it needs a voice. So this talks to businesses. It talks to people. And this voice is just getting louder, as we see more and more use cases come out there.

So have a couple examples. Salesforce (CRM) recently crossed the $100 million of AR for their product Agentforce. It's driving deals at ServiceNow (NOW), also crossing $100 million in AR. And even here in Canada, Thomson Reuters (TRI) is driving 20% of contract value and driving accelerated revenue growth.

Now, if you asked me last year, Where do you think we'd be in this cycle? I think we'd be further along in the benefit, and we'd see more dollars at play. But I think what's interesting is that the software companies are taking a page from the playbook of social media companies.

So what this is is giving away the product for free, getting people building around it, used to working with it, and then ultimately hooked on it, and then monetizing it later. So we see this with Salesforce with 4,000 paid customers out of 8,000 total AI customers. But only 800 of them are in production, so actually facing the customer is only 10%

Greg Bonnell - So if those are the three stages here, Where are we headed with it all? I think is the big question for people because a lot say there's a lot of opportunity here. It's going to do a lot of great things. A lot of people are worried about some of the things, too. What are these great promises?

Evan Chen - Yeah, let's dream the dream. Let's roll the clock forward five years. What does everything look like? I think we all have a data center in our phones. So something that would use to train the AI in a huge facility is now in our phone.

I think we're going to be talking to an AI more often than Elena from accounting. It's going to be working with you side by side. I think someone who has a business of 100 people before may only have 5 to 10 people, as they have trusted lieutenants doing the tasks that a team would before.

In dreaming this dream, I think it's tough to see exactly where it goes. But I would like to bring it back to maybe the mid '90s of the internet. So I was just getting born then, so I don't know exactly what happened.

Greg Bonnell - I remember. 14.4 dial-up modem. And even I was on it just before the Netscape browser came out. So you're hitting little dialogue STP boxes to chat with people. That's how rudimentary it was back in the day.

Evan Chen - Yeah, so let's just bring it back to the '90s. We didn't know exactly what was going to happen with the internet. You could talk to people. You could read the news instead of having a newspaper.

But if you fast-forward, what did it allow us to do? Connectivity. It led to the iPhone. It led to Uber, taxis from your phone. This didn't exist in the '90s, back when the internet was still around.

So I think it's going to be the same thing with AI. I just say to viewers, think big. Think bigger than even what you think this is going to be.

Now, some of the viewers may have heard we're not going to talk to Elena accounting anymore, or someone's going to be able to reduce their workforce from 20 to 30 people to 10 people. They said, well, I'm the human equation in all this.

Greg Bonnell - Let's talk about some of the risks here. I think some people are just worried about the disruptions to the labor market. And obviously some people have bigger concerns about AI.

Evan Chen - I think, for sure, disruption to the labor market is going to be something to watch. I think generally in history, though, people have found things to do. And what we were talking about earlier of these new business models that don't exist quite yet, I think there's a lot of opportunity there that we just don't know about for people to find other things to do.

More importantly, on the risks. I can't tell you exactly how this is going to work out. Think about every sci-fi novel. They had teleporters. They had cell phones, instant communication, like Zoom.

Well, we don't have teleporters, but we have Zoom. So I can't tell you the path of where we're going to go. But I think what's important for investors is to find out who the winners are, and then they will carry you forward.

So something interesting, what I think about the winners in this technological revolution, is unlike in previous cycles, I think the incumbents are the ones who are going to win. So the reason for that is I think they have all the right stuff here. So they have billions of dollars in CapEx on the ground, so those data centers we talked about. They have all this customer data for years and years of scraping whatever we do online.

And they have the right people. They have the smartest people working on this stuff. The only question mark here, I think, is attitude. Are they willing to disrupt themselves and their business model to do what it takes to come into this next layer of technology?

Original Post

data.title: Limoneira Aiming For Growth, But Results Are Likely Years Off
data.description:
Lemons hold a special place in culinary culture. I like to cook a lot of seafood, and cooking it in lemon juice is for many things almost obligatory, at least so far as I’m concerned. But we also just take lemons for granted, and never really think about how supplying them fits into the economy.

That’s where Limoneira (LMNR), our topic for today, comes in. One of the largest lemon growers in North America, and a couple big stories came out about them this week, so let’s give the humble lemon it’s day in the sun.

We’ll be talking both about their recent Q2 earnings release, which was a miss on revenue and EPS followed by lower guidance for production, and the news that they’re returning to a citrus partnership with Sunkist which they left back in 2010. We’ll be talking about both of those, as well as their aim to grow their avocado productions.

Limoneira: More Than Just Lemons

Wait, avocados? Weren’t we just talking about lemons? Yes we were, but Limoneira is more than just a lemon grower, though that’s their claim to fame. They also produce other citrus products and yes, grow a pretty large crop of avocados.

Limoneira has a surprisingly diverse business for a lemon farmer, because not only is it not just lemons, it’s not just farming either. Limoneira has a lot of land rights and water rights, and some of that’s in relatively high-value parts of California, so they have some real estate deals where they can flip the farmland for quite a bit more than its listed as being worth on the balance sheet.

Cash

$2.1 million

Total Current Assets

$29.8 million

Total Current Liabilities

$28.5 million

Long-Term Debt

$54.9 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$182.7 million

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

The current ratio is serviceable for an agribusiness, but nothing to write home about. The two things I want to talk about though are the bottom two numbers. Long-term debt isn’t huge for a company their size, but it has been growing. At the end of the fiscal year, they had $40 million in long-term debt, now it’s up to $54.9 million. That could be concerning.

On a happier note though, that total shareholder equity, which is largely from property and plants, is likely undervalued compared to what Limoneira could get on the real estate market if and when the developers come calling. It means their achievable price/book ratio is realistically even lower than it already is.

The price/book ratio at current prices is 1.64, which is already quite a bit lower than the sector median of 2.73. Assuming they can keep the debt from continuing to grow, this could be a first sign that Limoneira is a bit of a contrarian play.

The Struggles of 2025

But as I mentioned, Limoneira missed in Q2 earnings. After beating EPS estimates four of the last five quarters before that, this is a disappointing surprise. 2025 is shaping up to be a tough year for them.

(source: most recent 10-K and 10-Q from SEC)

You can see from here where Limoneira has been headed in the wrong direction, not just in Q2, but struggling for the past year and a half. They’re not really profitable right now, and that’s going to continue through this year if estimates to be believed, with non-GAAP EPS estimates of a 24¢ gain in Q3 and a 9¢ loss in Q4.

Revenue from their lemon business was down year over year in Q2, and where they’d previously offered a guidance of 5-5.5 million cartons this year, and now it’s down to 4.5-5 million cartons.

Avocados saw decreased production in Q2 year over year too, down from 1.6 million pounds in 2024 to 1.2 million pounds in 2025. That’s not a shock though, it’s reflective of biennial bearing of avocado trees, where they have a big crop one year and a small crop the next.

It came at a bad time though, because in 2024, they were only getting paid $1.47 per pound, and in 2025 they’ve been getting paid $2.26. The higher prices are a result of, among other things, the tariffs making imported avocados from Mexico more expensive. It was a particularly big deal around the Super Bowl, when avocado consumption in the US is highest.

Unfortunately, while the highest prices this year helped Limoneira increase its avocado revenue despite lower production, it happened in one of their off years for growing, and there’s no guarantee prices will remain high next year when their trees are producing more. Though tariffs probably will still be a thing, there is also the possibility that persistent higher prices will force people toward different snacks, ultimately lowering demand and normalizing prices.

Persistent Production Challenge: Growth Takes A While

While 2025 is likely to be a challenging year, it’s not as though Limoneira can expect a snap back year in 2026, either. More of their avocado trees will be in the high-yield year, so that segment should do better assuming prices are still strong, but their guidance for 2026 lemon production is even lower than the reduced guidance for 2025, down to 4-4.5 million cartons according to the conference call.

They’re also returning to a partnership in sales and marketing with Sunkist for citrus, which we’ll talk about later, and Limoneira says they expect it to improve margins, but reduce top-line revenue. If the avocado sector doesn’t strongly make up for this reduced production, 2026 might not be a lot better, and the EPS estimate for 2026 is still only 32¢, which is still nothing exciting at Limoneira’s current stock price. Even the ambitious 2027 estimate of 71¢ per share is just okay, and that undercuts the idea it would be a good contrarian pick, as I first thought.

Growth is the key here, but there’s an obvious problem: growth doesn’t just grow on trees, or rather the issue is it does just grow on trees. Limoneira is looking to grow their avocado production substantially, but per the conference call those trees aren’t going to be mature enough until the end of 2027. That means we won’t be seeing the results of those efforts for years.

The Return to Sunkist

Then we come to Sunkist. Limoneira had a long, long history of working with Sunkist in the citrus business, but in 2010 they ended that partnership, saying they would market their own lemons, and with multiple lemon brands they owned outright, they were highly confident.

Limoneira didn’t fall flat on their face with that, but here we are, 15 years later, and they announced earlier this week that they were re-merging their citrus sale and marketing operations with Sunkist. Though we don’t have all the details of this deal, it has the appearance of a quo ante bellum deal – that is they’re going right back to where they were in 2009.

In the conference call they conceded that top-line revenue would be down because of the merger, but predicted saving some $5 million in cost savings. It’s not clear what that would translate to on the bottom line, given the lower revenue.

While they insist in the call that this merger allows them to meet “several key objectives” and insisted “we’re not changing direction,” it certainly gives the appearance that they very much are changing direction.

We’ll have to wait to see what happens here, but I believe it’s fairly obviously them rethinking their decision to try to go it alone on lemons after trying it in 2010. They also told a story about Oxnard Lemon in the conference call which tried to justify the move, but I think raises more questions than it answers.

Limoneira acquired Oxnard Lemon, a full-line lemon packer, in 2018. But then they divested the wash and storage capacity, something they described themselves as being “very fortunate” to be able to do. After that, they ended up having to lease back that capacity at a much higher than expected price. It frankly doesn’t sound fortunate to me the way they tell it.

The argument they’re making is that with access to Sunkist’s infrastructure they won’t have that kind of problem in the future, but it feels like this was largely a self-created problem that they’re trying to solve by reversing another of the actions they had second thoughts about, splitting up their partnership with Sunkist. It makes me question the decision-making that underpins all of this.

No Rush to Buy Here

In the end, Limoneira does have some value locked away here, primarily in their real estate holdings and their efforts to grow avocado production. I still would be in no rush here, so I’m rating them a hold.

Limoneira’s effort to grow avocado production is going to take years to fully realize, the real estate profit is possible, but the timing there is uncertain. As such, even though the stock is around its 52-week low, I don’t see any obvious catalysts in 2025 or 2026. This is a long-term play, and prices might get lower still between now and when those avocado trees start maturing.

Investors should keep a close eye on that Sunkist merger, because that’s the more immediate matter. The $5 million cost savings will be welcome surely, but depending on how much it drags revenue down it might not result in substantial, meaningful GAAP earnings. With a 7.5¢ quarterly dividend, they too end up with a lower yield than the sector median, so even for the buy and hold crowd, there are likely better options out there at these prices.
